caveats: a: BLS covers technicians across wired and wireless telecommunications and contractors, not the full 517111 occupation mix.
a: GSMA tracks global operators and publicly announced deployments rather than wage-weighted U.S. lower-middle-market tasks.
a: The frozen compensation ratio is an ancestor-code estimate for all 517 and may misstate 517111 labor intensity.
rho: Publicly announced deployments may overrepresent successful projects and large operators.
rho: GSMA reports deployment counts and maturity, not the share of exposed labor opportunity realized within five years.
rho: Small carriers may buy proven vendor features faster than large carriers but have less integration staff and weaker data.
e: The official activity definition does not identify normalized EBITDA or transferable ownership.
e: The dataset has no defensible n-band value, so the size and composition of the actual LMM population are unknown.
e: Reported EBITDA can overstate transferable cash generation when network replacement, spectrum, pole, or expansion capital is treated separately.
s5: Fiber-industry expectations and one acquisitive public company do not measure an eligible-firm transfer probability.
s5: Network asset purchases and subscriber transfers may not qualify as company control transfers.
s5: The missing LMM denominator prevents direct conversion of observed deals into a probability.
q: Cogent's enterprise and carrier-focused mix is not representative of every residential or regional wired carrier.
q: A lower unit price can coexist with revenue growth as traffic, speed, and connection counts expand.
q: The cited sources do not measure savings-sharing after AI implementation.
d5: Fixed connection counts and fiber passings are not constant-quality service quantity for the entire wired-carrier basket.
d5: Trade-association deployment projections may be optimistic and may count multiple fiber passings of the same home.
d5: Fiber growth can accrue to large incumbents while LMM cable, copper, and video operators lose customers.
o: Continued demand for connectivity does not guarantee continued demand for the current wired operator or technology.
o: Wholesale and open-access models can separate physical network ownership from retail operator functions.
o: Fixed wireless and satellite may substitute more quickly in some geographies than the base assumes.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers generally pay recurring monthly subscription, per-connection, per-location, capacity, or metered bandwidth charges, with additional installation, equipment, usage, support, and wholesale fees. Residential contracts are often cancelable, while enterprise and carrier arrangements may have terms, minimums, service levels, and renewal repricing.
lens.excluded_activities: Telecommunications resellers without operated wired facilities, wireless carriers, satellite carriers other than the direct-to-home distribution exception included by NAICS, tower-only landlords, construction-only contractors, equipment manufacturers, captive networks, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the target normalized-EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market facilities-based wired carriers that repeatedly provide fiber, cable, copper, or mixed-network broadband, voice, video distribution, dedicated internet access, private network, optical transport, wavelength, and wholesale connectivity services to residential, business, carrier, or public-sector customers.

caveats: BLS covers technicians across wired and wireless telecommunications and contractors, not the full 517111 occupation mix.
GSMA tracks global operators and publicly announced deployments rather than wage-weighted U.S. lower-middle-market tasks.
The frozen compensation ratio is an ancestor-code estimate for all 517 and may misstate 517111 labor intensity.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task and time study from independent U.S. wired carriers, split across customer care, sales, billing, OSS/BSS, engineering, network operations, field installation and repair, construction, compliance, and management, with current automation identified, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Customer care, sales support, order entry, provisioning coordination, billing, collections, fraud review, outage communications, ticket triage, network monitoring, fault diagnosis, capacity analysis, documentation, and routine corporate work are exposed to AI assistance or avoidable hiring. Construction, splicing, premises installation, equipment replacement, emergency restoration, rights-of-way work, account negotiation, engineering accountability, and safety-critical network intervention remain substantially human. Existing network automation reduces the incremental not-yet-automated share.

d5.bridge: Source population/quantity: FCC data show U.S. residential fixed connections rising from about 112 million in 2020 to about 121 million in 2023, with FTTP up about 52%, fixed wireless up about 247%, copper down, and cable mixed; the Fiber Broadband Association reports 11.8 million 2025 U.S. fiber passings, while CTIA reports rapid fixed-wireless subscriber gains and cable losses. Transformation: combined overall connection growth, fiber expansion, enterprise data demand, and legacy-service decline into a moderated 3% five-year real quantity base for the mixed U.S. LMM wired-carrier basket. Error direction: passings and traffic growth can overstate paid LMM quantity, while speed and bandwidth improvements at flat prices can make connection counts understate service delivered.
caveats: Fixed connection counts and fiber passings are not constant-quality service quantity for the entire wired-carrier basket.
Trade-association deployment projections may be optimistic and may count multiple fiber passings of the same home.
Fiber growth can accrue to large incumbents while LMM cable, copper, and video operators lose customers.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-price five-year panel for 517111 connections, bandwidth delivered, route miles, fiber passings and take rates, enterprise circuits, voice and video lines, churn, new builds, decommissions, and wholesale traffic, separated from price and acquisitions, would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Total fixed residential connections and fiber connections have grown, and fiber deployment reached another record in 2025. The current basket is heterogeneous: fiber, enterprise transport, and dedicated access can expand, while copper voice, DSL, and legacy video shrink and cable faces fiber and fixed-wireless substitution. A near-flat to modest-growth real quantity base therefore combines strong data demand with technology migration and competitive losses.

caveats: The official activity definition does not identify normalized EBITDA or transferable ownership.
The dataset has no defensible n-band value, so the size and composition of the actual LMM population are unknown.
Reported EBITDA can overstate transferable cash generation when network replacement, spectrum, pole, or expansion capital is treated separately.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of 517111 carriers with normalized EBITDA, legal ownership, technology mix, recurring revenue, maintenance and growth capital, subsidy obligations, customer concentration, network ownership, franchise and license transferability, and enterprise value would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: The official definition already centers on operated wired facilities serving external customers, and recurring connectivity revenue fits the service lens. Eligibility is reduced by incumbents too large for the target band, municipal and cooperative ownership, captive or wholesale-only assets, customer and geography concentration, grant obligations, heavy maintenance capital, distressed legacy copper or video exposure, lease and interconnection dependence, and the declared absence of a defensible LMM firm count.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 517 ($71,420,936,579) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 517 ($668,853,509,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 517. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.3470 (margins.json[517]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Telecom. Services EBITDA/Sales 34.70%); revenue/employee $711,427 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 517); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: Continued demand for connectivity does not guarantee continued demand for the current wired operator or technology.
Wholesale and open-access models can separate physical network ownership from retail operator functions.
Fixed wireless and satellite may substitute more quickly in some geographies than the base assumes.
o.change_evidence: Cohort evidence on wired-carrier services eliminated or replaced by customer self-service, neutral-host platforms, wireless or satellite substitution, including retained network ownership, staffing, incidents, service quality, and reintermediation, would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Customers can self-install some equipment and software can automate provisioning, monitoring, support, and routing decisions, but an accountable carrier still must operate physical routes, switching and optical equipment, interconnections, billing, security, restoration, service levels, franchises, and regulatory obligations. Operator requirement falls where copper voice, linear video, or simple support disappears and where wireless, satellite, wholesale access, or customer-managed networking substitutes for the current wired basket.

caveats: Cogent's enterprise and carrier-focused mix is not representative of every residential or regional wired carrier.
A lower unit price can coexist with revenue growth as traffic, speed, and connection counts expand.
The cited sources do not measure savings-sharing after AI implementation.
q.change_evidence: Matched contracts and contribution margins before and after automation, including renewal prices, bandwidth units, churn, service credits, capex, vendor costs, headcount, and customer-service outcomes, would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Recurring subscriptions and per-connection or per-location contracts do not automatically fall when customer care, provisioning, or network operations become more productive, allowing some retained benefit. Retention is limited by renewal repricing, bandwidth price compression, promotional competition, service-level commitments, wholesale pass-through, required speed upgrades, customer churn, vendor fees, and the tendency to reinvest savings in capacity, reliability, cybersecurity, and customer experience. Cogent's recurring revenue and sharp price-per-megabit decline illustrate both sides.

rho.bridge: Source population/quantity: GSMA Intelligence tracks publicly announced AI deployments across more than 760 operators in 240 countries; its Q3 2025 dataset reports customer care as 47% of deployments, networks 17%, 67% of recorded deployments live, and small and medium operators averaging fewer deployments than large operators. Transformation: used the demonstrated live deployment base and concentration in relevant workflows to anchor a 70% five-year realization share of the packet's narrower exposed opportunity for U.S. LMM wired carriers, discounted for legacy integration, reliability, security, skills, and scale. Error direction: announcement bias and large global operators can overstate LMM realization, while mature vendor products and narrower small-carrier workflows can make the low case conservative.
caveats: Publicly announced deployments may overrepresent successful projects and large operators.
GSMA reports deployment counts and maturity, not the share of exposed labor opportunity realized within five years.
Small carriers may buy proven vendor features faster than large carriers but have less integration staff and weaker data.
rho.change_evidence: Audited before-and-after AI deployments at independent U.S. wired carriers measuring labor hours, first-contact resolution, truck rolls, mean time to repair, outages, billing errors, churn, security incidents, and total implementation cost by workflow would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Digitized customer, billing, ticket, telemetry, configuration, and network data make exposed workflows operationally implementable within five years. GSMA Intelligence documents live telco AI across customer care, networks, sales, and corporate functions, with cost savings the dominant objective. Realization is constrained by legacy OSS/BSS, vendor fragmentation, small-carrier resources, cybersecurity, service reliability, model error, union and workforce change, customer escalation, regulation, and the need for human authority over production networks.

s5.bridge: Source population/quantity: the Fiber Broadband Association's provider survey expects continued private-equity and M&A investment, while Cogent describes its acquired fiber business and a management history spanning multiple significant acquisitions; the FCC separately monitors merger effects in communications markets. Transformation: treated these directional deal-flow indicators as support for an 18% five-year qualifying control-transfer estimate among otherwise eligible U.S. LMM wired carriers, after excluding asset-only, oversized, municipal, cooperative, failed, and non-control events. Error direction: visible public and growth-fiber activity likely overstates qualifying LMM company sales, while private rural and regional transactions omitted from public indicators could make the low case conservative.
caveats: Fiber-industry expectations and one acquisitive public company do not measure an eligible-firm transfer probability.
Network asset purchases and subscriber transfers may not qualify as company control transfers.
The missing LMM denominator prevents direct conversion of observed deals into a probability.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year closed-deal census for U.S. 517111 carriers, linked to the eligible-firm denominator and classified by control sale, merger, asset purchase, bankruptcy, internal reorganization, buyer type, size, and regulatory completion, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Fiber deployment surveys anticipate continued private-equity and M&A activity, and a public competitive carrier reports integrating a major acquired fiber business after a history of acquisitions. Qualifying completion is discounted for regulatory and franchise approvals, subsidized-network obligations, financing conditions, valuation gaps, municipal or cooperative ownership, integration risk, and transactions that transfer only routes, subscribers, or operating assets rather than control of an eligible firm.

principal_driver: The principal driver is recurring connectivity demand combined with AI-exposed customer, billing, provisioning, and network-assurance workflows.
principal_weakness: The principal weakness is capital-heavy technology migration under strong price and substitution pressure, compounded by no defensible LMM firm-count anchor.

title: Wired Telecommunications Carriers
